{{short description |Hypothesis on the interstellar spreading of primordial life}}
[[File:Panspermie.svg |thumb |300px |Panspermia proposes that [[organism]]s such as [[bacteria]], complete with their [[DNA]], could be transported by means such as [[comet]]s through space to planets including [[Earth]].]]
'''Panspermia''' ({{ety |grc |''πᾶν'' (pan) |all | |''σπέρμα'' (sperma) |seed}}) is the [[hypothesis]], first proposed in the 5th century BCE by the Greek philosopher [[Anaxagoras]], that [[life]] exists throughout the [[Universe]], distributed by [[space dust]],<ref name="ARX-20171106">{{cite journal |last=Berera |first=Arjun |title=Space dust collisions as a planetary escape mechanism |journal=Astrobiology |volume=17 |issue=12 |pages=1274–1282 |date=6 November 2017 |arxiv=1711.01895 |bibcode=2017AsBio..17.1274B |doi=10.1089/ast.2017.1662 |pmid=29148823 |s2cid=126012488 }}</ref> [[meteoroid]]s,<ref name="SA-20180110">{{cite journal |last=Chan |first=Queenie H. S. |display-authors=etal |title=Organic matter in extraterrestrial water-bearing salt crystals |date=10 January 2018 |journal=[[Science Advances]] |volume=4 |pages=eaao3521 |issue=1 |doi=10.1126/sciadv.aao3521 |pmid=29349297 |pmc=5770164 |bibcode=2018SciA....4O3521C }}</ref> [[asteroids]], [[comets]],<ref name="cometary panspermia">{{cite journal |last=Wickramasinghe |first=Chandra |title=Bacterial morphologies supporting cometary panspermia: a reappraisal |journal=International Journal of Astrobiology |date=2011 |volume=10 |issue=1 |pages=25–30 |doi=10.1017/S1473550410000157 |bibcode=2011IJAsB..10...25W |citeseerx=10.1.1.368.4449 |s2cid=7262449}}</ref> and [[Small Solar System body |planetoids]],<ref>{{cite journal |last=Rampelotto |first=P. H. |year=2010 |title=Panspermia: A promising field of research |journal=Astrobiology Science Conference |volume=1538 |page=5224 |bibcode=2010LPICo1538.5224R |url=http://www.lpi.usra.edu/meetings/abscicon2010/pdf/5224.pdf}}</ref> as well as by [[spacecraft]] carrying unintended [[interplanetary contamination|contamination]] by [[microorganisms]].<ref name="NAT-20140519">Forward planetary contamination like ''[[Tersicoccus phoenicis]]'', that has shown resistance to methods usually used in [[Cleanroom|spacecraft assembly clean rooms]]: {{cite journal |last=Madhusoodanan |first=Jyoti |title=Microbial stowaways to Mars identified |date=May 19, 2014 |journal=[[Nature (journal) |Nature]] |doi=10.1038/nature.2014.15249 |s2cid=87409424 }}</ref><ref name="NASA-20131106">{{cite news |url=http://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/news.php?release=2013-319 |title=Rare New Microbe Found in Two Distant Clean Rooms |work=[[NASA]].gov |first=Guy |last=Webster |date=November 6, 2013 |access-date=November 6, 2013}}</ref><ref name="PHYS-20180227">{{cite web |author=Staff – [[Purdue University]] |title=Tesla in space could carry bacteria from Earth |url=https://phys.org/news/2018-02-tesla-space-bacteria-earth.html |date=27 February 2018 |work=[[phys.org]] |access-date=28 February 2018 }}</ref> Panspermia is a [[Fringe science |fringe theory]] with little support amongst mainstream scientists.<ref name="May 2019">{{Cite book |last=May |first=Andrew |title=Astrobiology: The Search for Life Elsewhere in the Universe |date=2019 |isbn=978-1785783425 |location=London |oclc=999440041 |quote=Although they were part of the scientific establishment—Hoyle at Cambridge and Wickramasinghe at the University of Wales—their views on the topic were far from mainstream, and panspermia remains a fringe theory}}</ref> Critics argue that it does not answer the question of the [[origin of life]] but merely places it on another celestial body. It is also criticized because it cannot be tested experimentally.<ref name='Experimental methods'/>

Panspermia proposes (for example) that [[microscopic life]]forms which can survive the effects of space (such as [[extremophile]]s) can become trapped in [[Ejecta |debris ejected]] into space after collisions between planets and [[small Solar System bodies]] that harbor life.<ref name="TNY-20190708">{{cite magazine |last=Chotiner |first=Isaac |title=What If Life Did Not Originate on Earth? |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/q-and-a/what-if-life-did-not-originate-on-earth |date=8 July 2019 |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |access-date=10 July 2019}}</ref> Panspermia studies concentrate not on how life began, but on methods that may distribute it in the Universe.<ref name="Wesson">A variation of the panspermia hypothesis is '''necropanspermia''' which astronomer Paul Wesson describes as follows: "The vast majority of organisms reach a new home in the Milky Way in a technically dead state … Resurrection may, however, be possible." {{cite magazine |last=Grossman |first=Lisa |title=All Life on Earth Could Have Come From Alien Zombies |magazine=[[Wired (magazine) |Wired]] |date=2010-11-10 |url=https://www.wired.com/wiredscience/2010/11/necropanspermia/ |access-date=10 November 2010}}</ref><ref name="Hoyle, F 1981. pp35-49">Hoyle, F. and Wickramasinghe, N.C. (1981). ''Evolution from Space''. Simon & Schuster, New York, and J.M. Dent and Son, London (1981), ch. 3 pp. 35–49.</ref><ref name="Wickramasinghe, J. 2010. pp. 137-154">Wickramasinghe, J., Wickramasinghe, C. and Napier, W. (2010). [http://alpha.sinp.msu.ru/~panov/LibBooks/LIFE/10972__legalreads.com.pdf ''Comets and the Origin of Life'']. World Scientific, Singapore. ch. 6 pp. 137–154. {{ISBN |978-9812566355}}</ref>

[[Pseudo-panspermia]] (sometimes called ''soft panspermia'' or ''molecular panspermia'') is the well-attested <!-- i.e. this bit is normal science --> hypothesis that many of the pre-biotic organic building-blocks of life originated in space, became incorporated in the solar nebula from which planets condensed, and were further—and continuously—distributed to planetary surfaces where [[abiogenesis|life then emerged]].<ref name="Klyce 2001">{{cite web |url=http://www.panspermia.org/oseti.htm |title=Panspermia Asks New Questions |access-date=25 July 2013 |last=Klyce |first=Brig |date=2001}}</ref><ref name="SETI 2001">{{cite book |doi=10.1117/12.435366 |chapter=Panspermia asks new questions |title=The Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI) in the Optical Spectrum III |journal=Proc. SPIE |series=The Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI) in the Optical Spectrum III |date=2001 |editor1-last=Kingsley |editor1-first=Stuart A |last1=Klyce |first1=Brig |editor2-last=Bhathal |editor2-first=Ragbir |volume=4273 |pages=11–14 |bibcode=2001SPIE.4273...11K |s2cid=122849901 }}</ref>

== History ==

The first mention of panspermia was in the writings of the fifth-century BC [[Ancient Greece|Greek]] philosopher [[Anaxagoras]].<ref name="AST-20200713">{{cite book |last1=Kolb |first1=Vera M. |last2=Clark III |first2=Benton C. |title=Astrobiology for a General Reader: A Question and Answers – Panspermia hypothesis |chapter-url=https://www.google.com/books/edition/Astrobiology_for_a_General_Reader/HFLwDwAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=Anaxagoras+Panspermia&pg=PA49&printsec=frontcover |date=2020 |chapter=10 |page=47 |publisher=[[Cambridge Scholars Publishing]] |isbn=978-1527555020 |accessdate=3 May 2022 }} The Panspermia hypothesis states that life exists elsewhere in the universe, and could be distributed far and wide. This idea was first introduced by the ancient Greek philosopher Anaxagoras (5th Century BCE), who believed that the universe is made of an infinite number of seeds ("spermata" in Greek). Upon reaching the Earth, these seeds gave rise to life. Anaxagorus introduced the term "Panspermia", which in Greek means literally "seeds everywhere".</ref> Panspermia began to assume a more scientific form through the proposals of [[Jöns Jacob Berzelius]] (1834),<ref>{{cite journal |last=Berzelius |first=J. J. |year=1834 |title=Analysis of the Alais meteorite and implications about life in other worlds |journal=Liebigs Annalen der Chemie und Pharmacie |volume=10 |pages=134–135}}</ref> Hermann E. Richter (1865),<ref>{{cite book |last1=Rothschild |first1=Lynn J. |author-link=Lynn J. Rothschild |last2=Lister |first2=Adrian M. |title=Evolution on Planet Earth – The Impact of the Physical Environment |publisher=Academic Press |date=June 2003 |pages=109–127 |isbn=978-0125986557 }}</ref> [[William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin |Kelvin]] (1871),<ref>{{cite journal |last=Thomson (Lord Kelvin) |first=W. |title=Inaugural Address to the British Association Edinburgh. 'We must regard it as probably to the highest degree that there are countless seed-bearing meteoritic stones moving through space.' |journal=Nature |volume=4 |pages=261–278 [262] |date=1871 |doi=10.1038/004261a0 |issue=92 |bibcode=1871Natur...4..261. |pmc=2070380 }}</ref> [[Hermann von Helmholtz]] (1879)<ref>{{cite news |title=The word: Panspermia |journal=New Scientist |date=7 March 2006 |issue=2541 |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/mg18925411.900-the-word-panspermia.html |access-date=25 July 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.panspermia-theory.com/home-page/history-of-panspermia |title=History of Panspermia |access-date=25 July 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20141013165725/http://www.panspermia-theory.com/home-page/history-of-panspermia |archive-date=13 October 2014 }}</ref> and finally reaching the level of a detailed scientific [[hypothesis]] through the efforts of the Swedish chemist [[Svante Arrhenius]] (1903).<ref>{{cite book |last=Arrhenius |first=S. |year=1908 |title=Worlds in the Making: The Evolution of the Universe |url=https://archive.org/details/worldsinmakinge00arrhgoog |location=New York |publisher=Harper & Row |bibcode=1908wmeu.book.....A }}</ref>

[[Fred Hoyle]] (1915–2001) and [[Chandra Wickramasinghe]] (born 1939) were influential proponents of panspermia.<ref name=Pollination>{{cite journal |last=Napier |first=W.M. |title=Pollination of exoplanets by nebulae |journal=International Journal of Astrobiology |date=2007 |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=223–228 |bibcode=2007IJAsB...6..223N |doi=10.1017/S1473550407003710 |s2cid=122742509}}</ref><ref name=Panspermia>{{cite journal |last=Line |first=M.A. |title=Panspermia in the context of the timing of the origin of life and microbial phylogeny |journal=Int. J. Astrobiol. |date=2007 |volume=6 |series=3 |pages=249–254 |bibcode=2007IJAsB...6..249L |doi=10.1017/S1473550407003813 |issue=3 |s2cid=86569201}}</ref> In 1974 they proposed the hypothesis that some [[Cosmic dust |dust in interstellar space]] was largely [[Organic compound |organic]] (containing carbon), which Wickramasinghe later proved to be correct.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/287518a0 |title=The 3.4-µm interstellar absorption feature |date=1980 |last1=Wickramasinghe |first1=D. T. |last2=Allen |first2=D. A. |journal=Nature |volume=287 |issue=5782 |pages=518–519 |bibcode=1980Natur.287..518W |s2cid=4352356 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/294239a0 |title=Diffuse interstellar absorption bands between 2.9 and 4.0 µm |date=1981 |last1=Allen |first1=D. A. |last2=Wickramasinghe |first2=D. T. |journal=Nature |volume=294 |issue=5838 |pages=239–240 |bibcode=1981Natur.294..239A |s2cid=4335356 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/BF00653492 |title=Three components of 3–4 μm absorption bands |date=1983 |last1=Wickramasinghe |first1=D. T. |last2=Allen |first2=D. A. |journal=Astrophysics and Space Science |volume=97 |issue=2 |pages=369–378 |bibcode=1983Ap&SS..97..369W |s2cid=121109158 }}</ref> Hoyle and Wickramasinghe further contended that life forms continue to enter the Earth's atmosphere, and may be responsible for epidemic outbreaks, new diseases, and the genetic novelty necessary for [[macroevolution]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Hoyle |first1=Fred |last2=Wickramasinghe |first2=Chandra |last3=Watson |first3=John |title=Viruses from Space and Related Matters |publisher=University College Cardiff Press |date=1986}}</ref>

== Overview == 

=== Core requirements ===

Panspermia requires:
# that organic molecules originated in space (perhaps to be distributed to Earth)
# that life originated from these molecules, extraterrestrially
# that this extraterrestrial life was transported to Earth.

The creation and distribution of organic molecules from space is now uncontroversial; it is known as pseudo-panspermia.<ref name="Klyce 2001"/> The existence of [[extraterrestrial life]] is unconfirmed but scientifically possible.<ref>{{cite news |first=John |last=Pickrell |title=Top 10: Controversial pieces of evidence for extraterrestrial life |date=4 September 2006 |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn9943-top-10-controversial-pieces-of-evidence-for-extraterrestrial-life.html |work=New Scientist |access-date=18 February 2011}}</ref> The transport of such life to Earth is considered pseudo-science.<ref name="May 2019"/>

=== Interstellar or interplanetary ===
[[File:ISS space station modules in the SSPF.jpg|thumb|Some microbes appear able to survive the [[planetary protection]] procedures applied to spacecraft in [[cleanroom]]s, intended to prevent accidental planetary contamination.<ref name="NAT-20140519"/><ref name="NASA-20131106"/>]]

Panspermia can be said to be either interstellar (between [[star system]]s) or interplanetary (between [[Planetary system |planets in the same star system]]).<ref name="LAT-20140308">{{cite news |last=Khan |first=Amina |title=Did two planets around nearby star collide? Toxic gas holds hints |url=https://www.latimes.com/science/sciencenow/la-sci-sn-beta-pictoris-star-planet-gas-collision-comets-carbon-monoxide-20140307,0,1884709.story |date=7 March 2014 |newspaper=[[LA Times]] |access-date=9 March 2014 }}</ref><ref name="SCI-20140306">{{cite journal |title=Molecular Gas Clumps from the Destruction of Icy Bodies in the β Pictoris Debris Disk |date=6 March 2014 |journal=[[Science (journal) |Science]] |volume=343 |issue=6178 |pages=1490–1492 |doi=10.1126/science.1248726 |pmid=24603151 |last1=Dent |first1=W. R. F. |last2=Wyatt |first2=M. C. |last3=Roberge |first3=A. |last4=Augereau |first4=J.- C. |last5=Casassus |first5=S. |last6=Corder |first6=S. |last7=Greaves |first7=J. S. |last8=De Gregorio-Monsalvo |first8=I. |last9=Hales |first9=A. |last10=Jackson |first10=A. P. |last11=Hughes |first11=A. M. |last12=Lagrange |first12=A.- M. |last13=Matthews |first13=B. |last14=Wilner |first14=D. |display-authors=3 |bibcode=2014Sci...343.1490D |arxiv=1404.1380 |s2cid=206553853 }}</ref> 

The major proposed mechanisms for panspermia are radiopanspermia, the propulsion of microbes through space by radiation pressure;<ref name="Arrhenius 1903"/> lithopanspermia, the transfer of organisms inside rocks, shielded from the space environment;<ref name=Mileikowsky/> and directed panspermia, managed deliberately to seed planetary systems with life.<ref name="Crick_Orgel"/>

[[Space probe]]s may be a viable transport mechanism for interplanetary cross-pollination within the Solar System. Space agencies have implemented [[planetary protection]] procedures to reduce the risk of planetary contamination,<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.aero.org/news/newsitems/sterilization073001.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060502194219/http://www.aero.org/news/newsitems/sterilization073001.html |archive-date=2006-05-02 |title=Studies Focus On Spacecraft Sterilization |publisher=The Aerospace Corporation |date=July 30, 2000}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.esa.int/esaMI/Aurora/SEMBJG9ATME_0.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120201224127/http://www.esa.int/esaMI/Aurora/SEMBJG9ATME_0.html |archive-date=2012-02-01 |title=Dry heat sterilisation process to high temperatures |publisher=European Space Agency |date=22 May 2006}}</ref> but microorganisms such as ''[[Tersicoccus phoenicis]]'' may be resistant to [[Cleanroom|spacecraft assembly cleaning]].<ref name="NAT-20140519"/><ref name="NASA-20131106"/>

== Origination and distribution of organic molecules: Pseudo-panspermia ==
{{main|Pseudo-panspermia}}

[[Pseudo-panspermia]] is the well-supported hypothesis that many of the small [[Organic compound|organic molecule]]s used for [[life]] originated in space, and were distributed to [[planet]]ary surfaces. Life then emerged on [[Earth]], and perhaps on other planets, by the processes of [[abiogenesis]].<ref name="Klyce 2001"/><ref name="SETI 2001"/> Evidence for pseudo-panspermia includes the discovery of organic compounds such as sugars, amino acids, and nucleobases in meteorites and other extraterrestrial bodies,<ref name="NASA-20191118">{{cite news |last1=Steigerwald |first1=Bill |last2=Jones |first2=Nancy |last3=Furukawa |first3=Yoshihiro |title=First Detection of Sugars in Meteorites Gives Clues to Origin of Life |url=https://www.nasa.gov/press-release/goddard/2019/sugars-in-meteorites |date=18 November 2019 |work=[[NASA]] |access-date=18 November 2019 }}</ref><ref name="PNAS-20191118">{{cite journal |last=Furukawa |first=Yoshihiro |display-authors=et al. |title=Extraterrestrial ribose and other sugars in primitive meteorites |date=18 November 2019 |journal=[[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America]] |volume=116 |issue=49 |pages=24440–24445 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1907169116 |pmid=31740594 |pmc=6900709 |bibcode=2019PNAS..11624440F |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="Furukawa Chikaraishi Ohkouchi 2019">{{Cite journal |last1=Furukawa |first1=Yoshihiro |last2=Chikaraishi |first2=Yoshito |last3=Ohkouchi |first3=Naohiko |last4=Ogawa |first4=Nanako O. |last5=Glavin |first5=Daniel P. |last6=Dworkin |first6=Jason P. |last7=Abe |first7=Chiaki |last8=Nakamura |first8=Tomoki |display-authors=3 |date=13 November 2019 |title=Extraterrestrial ribose and other sugars in primitive meteorites |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=116 |issue=49 |pages=24440–24445 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1907169116 |pmid=31740594 |bibcode=2019PNAS..11624440F |doi-access=free |pmc=6900709}}</ref><ref name="Martins Botta Fogel 2008">{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.epsl.2008.03.026 |title=Extraterrestrial nucleobases in the Murchison meteorite |date=2008 |last1=Martins |first1=Zita |last2=Botta |first2=Oliver |last3=Fogel |first3=Marilyn L. |author-link3=Marilyn Fogel |last4=Sephton |first4=Mark A. |last5=Glavin |first5=Daniel P. |last6=Watson |first6=Jonathan S. |last7=Dworkin |first7=Jason P. |last8=Schwartz |first8=Alan W. |last9=Ehrenfreund |first9=Pascale |display-authors=3 |journal=Earth and Planetary Science Letters |volume=270 |issue=1–2 |pages=130–136 |bibcode=2008E&PSL.270..130M |arxiv=0806.2286 |s2cid=14309508 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rivilla |first1=Víctor M. |last2=Jiménez-Serra |first2=Izaskun |last3=Martín-Pintado |first3=Jesús |last4=Colzi |first4=Laura |last5=Tercero |first5=Belén |last6=de Vicente |first6=Pablo |last7=Zeng |first7=Shaoshan |last8=Martín |first8=Sergio |last9=García de la Concepción |first9=Juan |last10=Bizzocchi |first10=Luca |last11=Melosso |first11=Mattia |date=2022 |title=Molecular Precursors of the RNA-World in Space: New Nitriles in the G+0.693−0.027 Molecular Cloud |journal=Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences |volume=9 |page=876870 |doi=10.3389/fspas.2022.876870 |arxiv=2206.01053 |bibcode=2022FrASS...9.6870R |issn=2296-987X|doi-access=free }}</ref> and the formation of similar compounds in the laboratory under outer space conditions.<ref name="NASA-20150303">{{cite web |last=Marlaire |first=Ruth |title=NASA Ames Reproduces the Building Blocks of Life in Laboratory |url=http://www.nasa.gov/content/nasa-ames-reproduces-the-building-blocks-of-life-in-laboratory |date=3 March 2015 |work=[[NASA]] |access-date=5 March 2015 }}</ref><ref name="Krasnokutski Chuang Jäger 2022">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/s41550-021-01577-9 |title=A pathway to peptides in space through the condensation of atomic carbon |date=2022 |last1=Krasnokutski |first1=S.A. |last2=Chuang |first2=K. J. |last3=Jäger |first3=C. |last4=Ueberschaar |first4=N. |last5=Henning |first5=Th. |display-authors=3 |journal=Nature Astronomy |volume=6 |issue=3 |pages=381–386 |arxiv=2202.12170 |bibcode=2022NatAs...6..381K |s2cid=246768607 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sithamparam |first1=Mahendran |last2=Satthiyasilan |first2=Nirmell |last3=Chen |first3=Chen |last4=Jia |first4=Tony Z. |last5=Chandru |first5=Kuhan |date=2022-02-11 |title=A material‐based panspermia hypothesis: The potential of polymer gels and membraneless droplets |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/bip.23486 |journal=Biopolymers |volume=113 |issue=5 |pages=e23486 |doi=10.1002/bip.23486 |pmid=35148427 |arxiv=2201.06732 |s2cid=246016331}}</ref> A prebiotic polyester system has been explored as an example.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Chandru |last2=Mamajanov |last3=Cleaves |last4=Jia |date=2020-01-19 |title=Polyesters as a Model System for Building Primitive Biologies from Non-Biological Prebiotic Chemistry |journal=Life |volume=10 |issue=1 |pages=6 |doi=10.3390/life10010006 |pmid=31963928 |pmc=7175156 |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jia |first1=Tony Z. |last2=Chandru |first2=Kuhan |last3=Hongo |first3=Yayoi |last4=Afrin |first4=Rehana |last5=Usui |first5=Tomohiro |last6=Myojo |first6=Kunihiro |last7=Cleaves |first7=H. James |date=2019-08-06 |title=Membraneless polyester microdroplets as primordial compartments at the origins of life |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |volume=116 |issue=32 |pages=15830–15835 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1902336116 |pmc=6690027 |pmid=31332006 |bibcode=2019PNAS..11615830J |doi-access=free }}</ref>
<!-- Please do not add material here, this is just a summary of the [[Pseudo-panspermia]] article. -->

==Radiopanspermia==

=== Hypothesis ===

In 1903, [[Svante Arrhenius]] proposed radiopanspermia, that microscopic forms of life can be propagated in space, driven by the [[radiation pressure]] from stars.<ref name="Arrhenius 1903">{{cite magazine |author-link=Svante Arrhenius |first=Svante |last=Arrhenius |title=Die Verbreitung des Lebens im Weltenraum |trans-title=The Distribution of Life in Space |magazine=Die Umschau |year=1903 |language=de}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Nicholson |first=Wayne L. |year=2009 |title=Ancient micronauts: Interplanetary transport of microbes by cosmic impacts |journal=Trends in Microbiology |volume=17 |issue=6 |pages=243–250 |doi=10.1016/j.tim.2009.03.004 |pmid=19464895}}</ref> Arrhenius argued that particles at a critical size below 1.5&nbsp;μm would be propelled at high speed by radiation pressure of the Sun. However, because its effectiveness decreases with increasing size of the particle, this mechanism holds for very tiny particles only, such as single bacterial spores.<ref name='Gerda Horneck'>{{cite journal |doi=10.1128/MMBR.00016-09 |title=Space Microbiology |date=2010 |last1=Horneck |first1=G. |last2=Klaus |first2=D. M. |last3=Mancinelli |first3=R. L. |journal=Microbiology and Molecular Biology Reviews |volume=74 |pages=121–156 |pmid=20197502 |issue=1 |pmc=2832349 |bibcode=2010MMBR...74..121H }}</ref>

=== Counter-arguments===

The main criticism of radiopanspermia came from [[Iosif Shklovsky]] and [[Carl Sagan]], who pointed out the evidence for the lethal action of space radiation ([[UV]] and [[X-ray]]s) in the cosmos.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Shklovskii |first1=I. S. |last2=Sagan |first2=Carl |title=Intelligent Life in the Universe |publisher=Emerson-Adams Press |date=1966 |isbn=978-1892803023}}{{page needed |date=May 2020}}</ref> Regardless of the evidence, Wallis and Wickramasinghe argued in 2004 that the transport of individual bacteria or clumps of bacteria, is overwhelmingly more important than lithopanspermia in terms of numbers of microbes transferred, even accounting for the death rate of unprotected bacteria in transit.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Wickramasinghe, M.K. |author2=Wickramasinghe, C. |title=Interstellar transfer of planetary microbiota |journal=Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society |date=2004 |volume=348 |issue=1 |pages=52–57 |bibcode=2004MNRAS.348...52W |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2966.2004.07355.x |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Data gathered by the orbital experiments [[Exobiology Radiation Assembly |ERA]], [[BIOPAN]], [[EXOSTACK]] and [[EXPOSE]] showed that isolated spores, including those of ''[[B. subtilis]]'', were rapidly killed <!-- by several orders of magnitude --> if exposed to the full space environment for merely a few seconds, but if shielded against solar [[UV]], the spores were capable of surviving in space for up to six years while embedded in clay or meteorite powder (artificial meteorites).<ref name='Gerda Horneck' /><ref name=Protection>{{cite journal |title=Protection of bacterial spores in space, a contribution to the discussion on panspermia |journal=Origins of Life and Evolution of the Biosphere |volume=31 |issue=6 |pages=527–547 |bibcode=2002ESASP.518..105R |pmid=11770260 |year=2001 |last1=Horneck |first1=G. |last2=Rettberg |first2=P. |last3=Reitz |first3=G. |last4=Wehner |first4=J. |last5=Eschweiler |first5=U. |last6=Strauch |first6=K. |last7=Panitz |first7=C. |last8=Starke |first8=V |last9=Baumstark-Khan |first9=C. |display-authors=3 |doi=10.1023/A:1012746130771 |s2cid=24304433 }}</ref>
Spores would therefore need to be heavily protected against UV radiation: exposure of unprotected DNA to solar UV and cosmic ionizing radiation would break it up into its constituent bases.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rahn |first1=R.O. |last2=Hosszu |first2=J.L. |year=1969 |title=Influence of relative humidity on the photochemistry of DNA films |journal=Biochim. Biophys. Acta |volume=190 |issue=1 |pages=126–131 |doi=10.1016/0005-2787(69)90161-0 |pmid=4898489}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Patrick |first1=M.H. |last2=Gray |first2=D.M. |year=1976 |title=Independence of photproduct formation on DNA conformation |journal=Photochem. Photobiol. |volume=24 |issue=6 |pages=507–513 |doi=10.1111/j.1751-1097.1976.tb06867.x |pmid=1019243 |s2cid=12711656 }}</ref><ref name='transport'>{{cite journal |title=The solar UV environment and bacterial spore UV resistance: considerations for Earth-to-Mars transport by natural processes and human spaceflight |journal=Mutation Research |date=21 January 2005 |first1=Wayne L. |last1=Nicholson |first2=Andrew C. |last2=Schuerger |first3=Peter |last3=Setlow |volume=571 |pages=249–264 |url=http://www.bi.ku.dk/dna/course/papers/F1b.nicholson.pdf |access-date=2 August 2013 |doi=10.1016/j.mrfmmm.2004.10.012 |pmid=15748651 |issue=1–2 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131228112459/http://www.bi.ku.dk/dna/course/papers/F1b.nicholson.pdf |archive-date=28 December 2013 |df=dmy-all}}</ref> Also, exposing DNA to the ultrahigh vacuum of space alone is sufficient to cause DNA damage, so the transport of unprotected DNA or RNA during interplanetary flights powered solely by light pressure is extremely unlikely.<ref name='transport'/>

The feasibility of other means of transport for the more massive shielded spores into the outer Solar System—for example, through gravitational capture by comets—is unknown. Rocks at least 1 meter in diameter are required to effectively shield resistant microorganisms, such as bacterial spores against galactic [[cosmic radiation]].<ref name='C. Clark'/><ref>{{cite journal |title=Natural transfer of microbes in space, part I: from Mars to Earth and Earth to Mars |journal=Icarus |volume=145 |issue=2 |pages=391–427 |pmid=11543506 |year=2000 |last1=Mileikowsky |first1=C. |last2=Cucinotta |first2=F.A. |last3=Wilson |first3=J.W. |last4=Gladman |first4=B. |last5=Horneck |first5=G. |last6=Lindegren |first6=L. |last7=Melosh |first7=J. |last8=Rickman |first8=H. |last9=Valtonen |first9=M. |last10=Zheng |first10=J.Q. |display-authors=3 |doi=10.1006/icar.1999.6317 |bibcode=2000Icar..145..391M}}</ref> These results clearly negate the radiopanspermia hypothesis.<ref name='Gerda Horneck' /><ref name=Protection />

== Lithopanspermia ==

=== Hypothesis ===

Lithopanspermia, the transfer of organisms in rocks from one planet to another either through interplanetary or interstellar space, such as in [[comet]]s or [[asteroid]]s,<ref name=Comets>{{cite news |last=Wall |first=Mike |title=Comet Impacts May Have Jump-Started Life on Earth |url=http://www.space.com/20102-comets-impact-earth-life.html |work=space.com |access-date=1 August 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Weber |first1=P |last2=Greenberg |title=Can spores survive in interstellar space? |journal=Nature |volume=316 |pages=403–407 |date=1985 |doi=10.1038/316403a0 |first2=J. M. |bibcode=1985Natur.316..403W |issue=6027 |s2cid=4351813 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last=Melosh |first=H. J. |title=The rocky road to panspermia |journal=Nature |volume=332 |pages=687–688 |date=1988 |doi=10.1038/332687a0 |pmid=11536601 |issue=6166 |bibcode=1988Natur.332..687M |s2cid=30762112 }}</ref><ref name=Mileikowsky>{{cite journal |title=Risks threatening viable transfer of microbes between bodies in our solar system |last1=Mileikowsky |first1=C. |last2=Cucinotta |first2=F. A. |last3=Wilson |first3=J. W. Wilson |author4=B. Gladman |author5=G. Horneck |author6=L. Lindegren |author7=J. Melosh |author8=Hans Rickman |author9=M. Valtonen |author10=J. Q. Zheng |display-authors=3 |journal=Planetary and Space Science |date=2000 |volume=48 |issue=11 |pages=1107–1115 |doi=10.1016/S0032-0633(00)00085-4 |bibcode=2000P&SS...48.1107M}}</ref> remains speculative.<ref name='Belbruno2012'>{{cite journal |title=Chaotic Exchange of Solid Material between Planetary |journal=Astrobiology |date=2012 |last1=Belbruno |first1=Edward |last2=Moro-Martı´n |first2=Amaya |last3=Malhotra |first3=Renu |last4=Savransky |first4=Dmitry |display-authors=3 |volume=12 |issue=8 |doi=10.1089/ast.2012.0825 |pages=754–774 |pmid=22897115 |pmc=3440031 |arxiv=1205.1059 |bibcode=2012AsBio..12..754B }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.princeton.edu/news/2012/09/24/slow-moving-rocks-better-odds-life-crashed-earth-space |title=Slow-moving rocks better odds that life crashed to Earth from space |publisher=Princeton University |date=September 24, 2012 |first=Morgan |last=Kelly}}</ref> A variant would be for organisms to travel between star systems on nomadic exoplanets or exomoons.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Sadlok |first=Grzegorz |date=2020-02-07 |title=On A Hypothetical Mechanism of Interstellar Life Transfer Trough Nomadic Objects |journal=Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres |doi=10.1007/s11084-020-09591-z |pmid=32034615 |bibcode=2020OLEB...50...87S |doi-access=free |volume=50 |issue=1–2 |pages=87–96}}</ref> 

Although there is no evidence that lithopanspermia has occurred in the Solar System, the various stages have become amenable to experimental testing.<ref name='Experimental methods'>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.mimet.2009.10.004 |title=Experimental methods for studying microbial survival in extraterrestrial environments |date=2010 |last1=Olsson-Francis |first1=Karen |last2=Cockell |first2=Charles S. |journal=Journal of Microbiological Methods |volume=80 |pages=1–13 |pmid=19854226 |issue=1}}</ref>

* '''Planetary ejection''' – For lithopanspermia to occur, microorganisms must survive ejection from a planetary surface, which involves extreme forces of acceleration and shock with associated temperature excursions. Hypothetical values of shock pressures experienced by ejected rocks are obtained with Martian meteorites, which suggest the shock pressures of approximately 5 to 55&nbsp;GPa, acceleration of 3&nbsp;Mm/s<sup>2</sup> and [[Jerk (physics) |jerk]] of 6&nbsp;Gm/s<sup>3</sup> and post-shock temperature increases of about 1 K to 1000 K. Some organisms appear able to survive these conditions.<ref name='Cockell 2008'>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s11084-007-9112-3 |pmid=17906941 |title=The Interplanetary Exchange of Photosynthesis |date=2007 |last1=Cockell |first1=Charles S. |journal=Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=87–104 |bibcode=2008OLEB...38...87C |s2cid=5720456 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1089/ast.2007.0134 |title=Microbial Rock Inhabitants Survive Hypervelocity Impacts on Mars-Like Host Planets: First Phase of Lithopanspermia Experimentally Tested |date=2008 |last1=Horneck |first1=Gerda |last2=Stöffler |first2=Dieter |last3=Ott |first3=Sieglinde |last4=Hornemann |first4=Ulrich |last5=Cockell |first5=Charles S. |last6=Moeller |first6=Ralf |last7=Meyer |first7=Cornelia |last8=De Vera |first8=Jean-Pierre |last9=Fritz |first9=Jörg |last10=Schade |first10=Sara |last11=Artemieva |first11=Natalia A. |display-authors=3 |journal=Astrobiology |volume=8 |pages=17–44 |pmid=18237257 |issue=1 |bibcode=2008AsBio...8...17H }}</ref> 

* '''Survival in transit''' – The survival of microorganisms has been studied extensively using both simulated facilities and in low Earth orbit. A large number of microorganisms have been selected for exposure experiments, both human-borne microbes (significant for future crewed missions) and [[extremophiles]] (significant for determining the physiological requirements of survival in space).<ref name="Experimental methods"/>

* '''Atmospheric entry''' – to test whether microbes on or within rocks could survive hypervelocity entry through Earth's atmosphere.<ref name='Cockell 2008'/> Tests could use sounding rockets and orbital vehicles.<ref name="Experimental methods"/><ref name='Cockell 2008'/> ''[[B. subtilis]]'' spores inoculated onto [[granite]] domes were twice subjected to hypervelocity atmospheric transit by launch to a ∼120&nbsp;km altitude on an Orion two-stage rocket. The spores survived on the sides of the rock, but not on the forward-facing surface that reached 145&nbsp;°C.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1089/ast.2005.5.726 |title=Bacillus subtilis Spores on Artificial Meteorites Survive Hypervelocity Atmospheric Entry: Implications for Lithopanspermia |date=2005 |last1=Fajardo-Cavazos |first1=Patricia |last2=Link |first2=Lindsey |last3=Melosh |first3=H. Jay |last4=Nicholson |first4=Wayne L. |journal=Astrobiology |volume=5 |issue=6 |pages=726–736 |pmid=16379527 |bibcode=2005AsBio...5..726F }}</ref> As photosynthetic organisms must be close to the surface of a rock to obtain sufficient light energy, atmospheric transit might act as a filter against them by ablating the surface layers of the rock. Although [[cyanobacteria]] can survive the desiccating, freezing conditions of space, the STONE experiment showed that they cannot survive atmospheric entry.<ref name='filter'>{{cite journal |doi=10.1089/ast.2006.0038 |title=Interplanetary Transfer of Photosynthesis: An Experimental Demonstration of a Selective Dispersal Filter in Planetary Island Biogeography |date=2007 |last1=Cockell |first1=Charles S. |display-authors=4 |last2=Brack |first2=André |last3=Wynn-Williams |first3=David D. |last4=Baglioni |first4=Pietro |last5=Brandstätter |first5=Franz |last6=Demets |first6=René |last7=Edwards |first7=Howell G.M. |last8=Gronstal |first8=Aaron L. |last9=Kurat |first9=Gero |last10=Lee |first10=Pascal |last11=Osinski |first11=Gordon R. |last12=Pearce |first12=David A. |last13=Pillinger |first13=Judith M. |last14=Roten |first14=Claude-Alain |last15=Sancisi-Frey |first15=Suzy |journal=Astrobiology |volume=7 |pages=1–9 |pmid=17407400 |issue=1 |bibcode=2007AsBio...7....1C }}</ref> Small non-photosynthetic organisms deep within rocks might survive the exit and entry process, including [[impact survival]].<ref>{{cite news |url=http://astrobiology.com/2013/09/could-life-have-survived-a-fall-to-earth.html |title=Could Life Have Survived a Fall to Earth? |work=EPSC |date=12 September 2013 |access-date=2015-04-21 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/2131136-microbes-might-thrive-after-crash-landing-on-board-a-meteorite/ |title=Microbes might thrive after crash-landing on board a meteorite |last=Boyle |first=Rebecca |date=2017-05-16 |website=New Scientist |access-date=2019-12-11}}</ref>

== Directed panspermia ==
{{main|Directed panspermia}}

=== Hypothesis ===

Directed panspermia would be the deliberate transport of microorganisms in space, sent to Earth to start life here, or sent from Earth to seed new planetary systems with life by [[introduced species]] of microorganisms on lifeless planets.<ref>{{cite book |last=Mautner |first=Michael N. |title=Seeding the Universe with Life: Securing Our Cosmological Future |location=Washington, DC |date=2000 |isbn=978-0476003309 |url=http://www.astro-ecology.com/PDFSeedingtheUniverse2005Book.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mautner |first1=M |last2=Matloff |title=Directed panspermia: A technical evaluation of seeding nearby planetary systems |journal=Journal of the British Interplanetary Society |volume=32 |pages=419 |date=1979 |first2=G. |url=http://www.astro-ecology.com/PDFDirectedPanspermia1JBIS1979Paper.pdf |bibcode=1979JBIS...32..419M }}</ref><ref name="autogenerated1">{{cite journal |last=Mautner |first=M. N. |title=Directed panspermia. 3. Strategies and motivation for seeding star-forming clouds |journal=Journal of the British Interplanetary Society |volume=50 |pages=93–102 |date=1997 |url=http://www.astro-ecology.com/PDFDirectedPanspermia3JBIS1997Paper.pdf |bibcode=1997JBIS...50...93M}}</ref><ref name="BBC-2011">{{cite web |title=Impacts 'more likely' to have spread life from Earth |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-14637109 |date=23 August 2011 |publisher=[[BBC]] |access-date=24 August 2011}}</ref>  The Nobel prize winner [[Francis Crick]], along with [[Leslie Orgel]] proposed that life may have been purposely spread by an advanced extraterrestrial civilization,<ref name="Crick_Orgel">{{cite journal |last1=Crick |first1=F. H. |last2=Orgel |first2=L. E. |title=Directed Panspermia |journal=Icarus |volume=19 |pages=341–348 |date=1973 |doi=10.1016/0019-1035(73)90110-3 |bibcode=1973Icar...19..341C |issue=3 |citeseerx=10.1.1.599.5067}}</ref> but considering an early "[[RNA world]]" Crick noted later that life may have originated on Earth.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Orgel |first1=Leslie E. |last2=Crick |first2=Francis H. |title=Anticipating an RNA world. Some past speculations on the origin of life: where are they today? |journal=The FASEB Journal |date=January 1993 |volume=7 |issue=1 |pages=238–239 |doi=10.1096/fasebj.7.1.7678564 |pmid=7678564 |s2cid=11314345 }}</ref><ref name='C. Clark'>{{cite journal |title=Planetary Interchange of Bioactive Material: Probability Factors and Implications |journal=Origins of Life and Evolution of the Biosphere |date=February 2001 |last=Clark |first=Benton C. |volume=31 |issue=1–2 |pages=185–197 |bibcode=2001OLEB...31..185C |doi=10.1023/A:1006757011007 |pmid=11296521 |s2cid=12580294 }}</ref> The astronomer [[Thomas Gold]] suggested in 1960 the hypothesis of "Cosmic Garbage", that life on Earth might have originated accidentally from a pile of [[waste]] products dumped on Earth long ago by extraterrestrial beings.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Gold |first=Thomas |title=Cosmic Garbage |journal=Air Force and Space Digest |volume=43 |issue=5 |date=May 1960 |pages=65 |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_air-force-magazine_1960-05_43_5/page/65/mode/2up}}</ref>

=== Counter-arguments ===

Directed panspermia could, in theory, be demonstrated by finding a distinctive 'signature' message had been deliberately implanted into either the [[genome]] or the [[genetic code]] of the first microorganisms by our hypothetical progenitor, some 4 billion years ago. It has been suggested that the [[Phi_X_174|bacteriophage φX174]] might represent such a message. However, there is no known mechanism that could prevent [[mutation]] and [[natural selection]] from removing such a message over long periods of time.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Message through time |author=Marx, G. |journal=Acta Astronautica |date=1979 |volume=6 |issue=1–2 |pages=221–225 |doi=10.1016/0094-5765(79)90158-9 |bibcode=1979AcAau...6..221M }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=Is bacteriophage φX174 DNA a message from an extraterrestrial intelligence? |last1=Yokoo |first1=H. |last2=Oshima |first2=T. |journal=Icarus |date=1979 |volume=38 |issue=1 |pages=148–153 |doi=10.1016/0019-1035(79)90094-0 |bibcode=1979Icar...38..148Y}}</ref><ref name="The New York Times">{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2007/06/26/science/26DNA.html |title=Human DNA, the Ultimate Spot for Secret Messages (Are Some There Now?) |last=Overbye |first=Dennis |date=26 June 2007 |access-date=2014-10-09 |newspaper=The New York Times }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last=Davies |first=Paul C.W. |title=The Eerie Silence: Renewing Our Search for Alien Intelligence |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |location=Boston|date=2010 |isbn=978-0547133249 |title-link=The Eerie Silence |page=   }}{{page needed |date=May 2020}}</ref>

== Hoaxes ==

A separate fragment of the [[Orgueil (meteorite) |Orgueil]] meteorite (kept in a sealed glass jar since its discovery) was found in 1965 to have a seed capsule embedded in it, while the original glassy layer on the outside remained undisturbed. Despite great initial excitement, the seed was found to be that of a European [[Juncaceae]] or Rush plant that had been glued into the fragment and camouflaged using [[coal dust]]. The outer "fusion layer" was in fact glue. While the perpetrator of this hoax is unknown, it is thought that they sought to influence the 19th-century debate on [[spontaneous generation]]—rather than panspermia—by demonstrating the transformation of inorganic to biological matter.<ref>{{cite journal |title=Contaminated Meteorite |journal=Science |volume=146 |issue=3648 |pages=1157–1161 |doi=10.1126/science.146.3648.1157 |pmid=17832241 |year=1964 |last1=Anders |first1=E. |last2=Dufresne |first2=E. R. |last3=Hayatsu |first3=R. |last4=Cavaille |first4=A. |last5=Dufresne |first5=A. |last6=Fitch |first6=F. W. |bibcode=1964Sci...146.1157A |s2cid=38428960}}</ref>

== See also ==

* {{annotated link |Abiogenesis}}
* {{annotated link |Astrobiology}}
* {{annotated link |Cryptobiosis}}
* {{annotated link |List of microorganisms tested in outer space}}
* {{annotated link |Planetary protection}}

== References ==
{{reflist}}

== Further reading ==
{{Library resources box}}

* {{citation |last=Crick |first=Francis |author-link=Francis Crick |title=Life, Its Origin and Nature |publisher=Simon & Schuster |year=1981 |isbn=978-0708822357 |ref=none}}
* {{citation |last=Hoyle |first=Fred |author-link=Fred Hoyle |title=The Intelligent Universe |publisher=Michael Joseph |place=London |year=1983 |isbn=978-0718122980 |ref=none}}

== External links ==

* Cox, Brian. [https://www.bbc.co.uk/ideas/videos/are-we-thinking-about-alien-life-all-wrong/p0b6vpk4 "Are we thinking about alien life all wrong?"]. BBC Ideas, video made by Pomona Pictures, 29 November 2021.
* Loeb, Abraham. [https://blogs.scientificamerican.com/observations/did-life-from-earth-escape-the-solar-system-eons-ago/ "Did Life from Earth Escape the Solar System Eons Ago?"]. ''[[Scientific American]]'', 4 November 2019
* Loeb, Abraham. [https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/noahs-spaceship/ "Noah's Spaceship"] ''[[Scientific American]]'', 29 November 2020

{{Astrobiology}}
{{Origin of life}}
{{Extraterrestrial life}}

[[Category:Panspermia |Panspermia ]]
[[Category:Astrobiology]]
[[Category:Origin of life]]
[[sn:Panspermia]][[Category:Biological hypotheses]]
[[Category:Prebiotic chemistry]]
[[Category:Fringe science]]
[[Category:Pages]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
